questions.question: Where do the three checkpoints sit in the request lifecycle?
options: All three run after the model finishes
Pre-gen runs on the prompt before the model, during-gen filters the stream, post-gen runs on the completed output
All three run before the model is called
Only post-gen runs; pre-gen and during-gen are aliases

questions.question: What is the goal of during-gen filtering?
options: To translate the output to another language
To buffer chunks and terminate the stream early when a known harmful continuation appears, before the user sees it
To increase token throughput
To replace the prompt with a refusal

questions.question: How does the aggregator decide the final action?
options: By averaging the confidences of every signal
By taking the maximum severity across detector, token-filter, classifier, and rules signals and mapping it to block, redact, warn, or allow
By calling the model a second time
By looking only at the rules engine

questions.question: Why does the gate emit a structured RequestTrace per request?
options: To slow the system down
To give a reviewer a per-checkpoint audit trail so a regression in any layer can be traced to the right module
To replace logging
To replicate the trace into the prompt

questions.question: What happens when pre-gen confidence exceeds the block threshold?
options: The model is still called for completeness
The gate returns the policy refusal and skips during-gen and post-gen entirely
The gate raises an exception
The gate lowers the threshold and retries

questions.question: Why does the demo intentionally let some encoding-trick prompts reach during-gen?
options: Because encoding tricks are not real attacks
Because the input pipeline does not always normalize them in time; the during-gen filter is the defense in depth that catches the leaked continuation
Because the model refuses them anyway
Because the rules engine handles encoding
